perturbation.original_text: All products and any residue thereof remaining after the tests are completed shall returned to the disclosing party no later than one hundred twenty (120) days from the effective date shown above, or shall be destroyed by the receiving party.
perturbation.changed_text: All products and any residue thereof remaining after the tests are completed shall be returned to the disclosing party no later than two hundred and seventy (270) days from the effective date shown above, or shall be destroyed by the receiving party.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the return deadline to 270 days may conflict with regulations regarding the destruction or return of sensitive materials within a reasonable timeframe, depending on the jurisdiction and type of materials. While not a direct contradiction of a specific statute without knowing the precise material and governing law, it introduces a potential inconsistency by extending the retention period significantly.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Potential conflict with environmental regulations or industry-specific guidelines regarding the handling and disposal of materials.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this law doesn't specify the exact timeframe for returning residue, extending it to 270 days might cause problems, because the rules for the disposal of waste and materials have to be complied with within a reasonable time after tests are complete. If the tests results are not given for several months, storing the waste for such a long period of time could violate Ontario Regulation 347/90 if the waste is considered hazardous.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the timeframe for returning residue from 120 to 270 days. The Law Explanation suggests this extension could violate Ontario Regulation 347/90 if the waste is considered hazardous because waste materials have to be disposed of within a 'reasonable time' after tests are complete. Scraped Law Snippet 1 is R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 347: GENERAL - WASTE MANAGEMENT, and details the regulation. The perturbation explanation also notes that a potential inconsistency is created by extending the retention period significantly. Therefore, the change introduces a potential inconsistency related to waste management regulations, which qualifies as a legal contradiction under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Any dispute in relation to this Agreement shall be resolved by the common court of the country of the first defendant, i.e. if suit is first brought by Add-X Biotech the dispute including all counterclaims shall be resolved by ………………. courts applying ……………. law and if suit is first brought by ………………………. the dispute including all counterclaims shall be resolved by Canadian courts applying Canadian law.
perturbation.changed_text: Any dispute in relation to this Agreement shall be resolved through mandatory arbitration in Sweden, regardless of where the suit is first brought. The arbitration decision shall be final and binding, with no right to appeal.
perturbation.explanation: Mandatory arbitration clauses, particularly those that eliminate the right to appeal, can be problematic under Canadian law, particularly if they are deemed unconscionable or unfair, or if they unduly restrict access to justice.  While arbitration is generally accepted, removing the right to appeal may violate principles of natural justice and procedural fairness.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Arbitration Act permits arbitration agreements, Section 46 allows for appeals of arbitral awards on questions of law, with leave of the court.  Completely removing the right to appeal, as in the modified text, could be challenged under the Act and general principles of fairness and access to justice.

perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates arbitration in Sweden, eliminating the right to appeal, regardless of where the suit is brought. The original text allowed for court resolution based on where the suit was first brought, offering avenues for appeal within that judicial system. The Law Explanation points out that Section 46 of the Arbitration Act permits appeals of arbitral awards on questions of law, with leave of the court. The change directly contradicts this potential avenue for appeal by explicitly stating 'no right to appeal.' While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides context that there is an 'Arbitration Act, 1991' which, as the Law Explanation states, provides for appeals under certain conditions, something that is explicitly removed by the modified text.

perturbation.original_text: Background Add-X Biotech AB and …..…………………………. have been discussing the possibility of a future business relationship concerning the Project (as defined below.) In order to facilitate the further discussion, the parties have agreed to disclose confidential information and to perform certain tests relating to the AddiFlex material.
perturbation.changed_text: Background Add-X Biotech AB and …..…………………………. have been discussing the possibility of a future business relationship concerning the Project (as defined below.) In order to facilitate the further discussion, the parties have agreed to disclose confidential information and to perform certain tests relating to the AddiFlex material. Add-X Biotech AB will not be held liable for any damages, including direct, indirect, incidental, special, or consequential damages arising out of or in any way connected with the use of disclosed information or the testing of the AddiFlex material, even if Add-X Biotech AB has been advised of the possibility of such damages.
perturbation.explanation: Introducing a blanket exclusion of liability for all types of damages, including those arising from gross negligence or willful misconduct, could be unenforceable under Canadian law.  While parties can limit liability, courts often scrutinize clauses that completely exclude liability, especially for serious breaches of duty.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Unfair Contract Terms Act (Hypothetical in Ontario Law)
perturbation.law_citation: Consumer Protection Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 30, Sched. A
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the Consumer Protection Act primarily addresses consumer transactions, it reflects a general principle against unconscionable terms. A blanket exclusion of liability, particularly one that covers gross negligence or willful misconduct, could be deemed unconscionable and unenforceable in a commercial context as well, if it significantly imbalances the parties' rights and obligations. Section 9 regarding future waiver of rights could be argued.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: g consumers the right to cancel contracts within a certain time period for several specified types of transactions, potentially including transactions entered into at home, and warranty and repair services contracts. [ 18 ] [ 19 ] Other states have been the leaders in specific aspects of consumer protection. For example, Florida, Delaware, and Minnesota have legislated requirements that contracts be written at reasonable readability levels as a large proportion of contracts cannot be understood by most consumers who sign them. [ 20 ] Considering the state of Massachusetts, the Massachusetts Consumer Protection Law, MGL 93A, clearly highlights the rights and violations of consumer protection law in the state. The chapter explains what actions are considered illegal under the law for which a party can seek monetary damages from the other party at fault. [ 21 ] Some examples of practices that constitute a Chapter 93A violation would be when: A Business charges a consumer higher rates than the marked price The refund policy is not clearly posted where it can be readily noticed and understood A business fails to tell you relevant information regarding your product or service misleads you in any way. [ 22 ] The laws under MGL 93A prohibit activities that relate to overpricing to a consumer and the use of " Bait and Switch " techniques. A court will award the plaintiff the damages if they can prove the (1) defendant knowingly and intentionally violated the MGL 93A agreement or (2) the defendant would not "grant relief in bad faith" knowing that the actions violated the MGL 93A agreement. [ 22 ] Additionally, failure to disclose refund/ return policy, warranties, and critical information about the product/service are all in violation of the legislation, and can result in triple damages and lawyer fees. Food and drug Pure Food and Drug Act – led to the creation of the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) to regulate foods, drugs, and more. Communications Communications Act of 1934 – created the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) to regulate all radio and interstate cable, phone, and satellite communications. Banking Fair Credit Reporting Act (FRCA) – regulates the collection, dissemination, and use of consumer credit information Fair Debt Collection Practices Act (FDCPA) – eliminates abusive consumer practices, ensures fairness, etc. Truth in Lending Act (TILA) – requires clear disclosure of key terms of the lending arrangement and all costs. Real estate Real Estate Settlement Procedures Act (RESPA) – prohibits kickbacks and requires lenders to provide a good faith estimate of costs Health insurance Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) – provides consumer protection for health information Digital media Digital Millennium Copyright Act – prohibits production or sale of devices or services intended to circumvent copyright measures. Digital Media Consumers' Rights Act (proposed) – would repeal the Digital Millennium Copyright Act Australia [ edit ] The Australian Consumer Law Division 2 of Part 2 of the Australian Securities and Investm
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a blanket exclusion of liability, which, as the Perturbation Explanation states, could be unenforceable under Canadian law, especially for gross negligence or willful misconduct. The Law Explanation also mentions a general principle against unconscionable terms, suggesting that a broad liability exclusion could be deemed unconscionable if it significantly imbalances the parties' rights. The provided scraped law snippet, while US focused, speaks to the unenforceability of certain consumer protections. Although this refers to consumer protection, the core legal principle is similar: a contract cannot unfairly waive rights, especially where there is a significant power imbalance.

perturbation.original_text: Each party may disclose to the other party confidential and/or proprietary information (hereinafter “the Information”) including but not limited to trade secrets, business practices and customer information. It is understood and agreed that these disclosures are made for the exclusive purpose of furthering the receiving party’s information about a possible business relationship concerning the AddiFlex® project (hereinafter “the Project”)
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may disclose to the other party confidential and/or proprietary information (hereinafter “the Information”) including but not limited to trade secrets, business practices and customer information. It is understood and agreed that these disclosures are made for any purpose that the receiving party deems appropriate, at their sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the purpose of the disclosure from 'furthering the receiving party's information about a possible business relationship' to 'any purpose that the receiving party deems appropriate' significantly broadens the scope of permissible use. This could violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in contracts under Canadian law.  Using the information for purposes completely unrelated to the original intent could be considered a breach.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada in *Bhasin v Hrynew* recognized an organizing principle of good faith in contractual performance, including a duty of honesty. Using confidential information for purposes entirely unrelated to the contemplated business relationship, as permitted by the modified clause, would likely violate this duty, even if technically within the 'sole discretion' of the receiving party. The modified clause allows bad faith use of Confidential Information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Tsilhqot’in Nation v. British Columbia - SCC Cases Mailing List RSS Feeds JSON Feeds Date modified: 2025-07-11
perturbation.justification: The modification allows the receiving party to use confidential information for 'any purpose,' which directly contradicts the implied duty of good faith. The Law Explanation states that this violates the duty of honesty as recognized in *Bhasin v Hrynew*. Although the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the Law Explanation referencing a Supreme Court of Canada case establishes a legal basis for the contradiction. The altered text creates an inconsistency that permits bad faith use, thus creating a legal contradiction.
